result.markdown: --- title: "[viz.theme_forest()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-function}" body-classes: doc-api-page page-navigation: false html-table-processing: none --- ::: {.doc-subject} A minimal plotnine theme for forest plots. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/viz/_forest.py#L103-L140){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python viz.theme_forest() ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Returns a composable plotnine theme object suitable for forest plots of hazard ratios or other contrasts. Horizontal grid lines are suppressed. The y-axis line is removed so the term labels stand alone. ::: ## Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [plotnine.theme]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A composable theme object. Add it to a `ggplot` with `+`. ::: ## Examples {.doc-examples} Apply the forest theme to a forest plot of Cox model hazard ratios: ::: {#e31de7ab .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) cox = gw.CoxPH().fit(y, lung[["age", "sex"]]) gw.plot_forest(cox, backend="plotnine") + gw.theme_forest() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ![](viz.theme_forest_files/figure-html/cell-2-output-1.png){width=672 height=480} ::: :::
